items.q: Is a coach's opinion of technique quantitative or qualitative data?
items.correct: Qualitative - it is descriptive, not numerical.
items.misconception: Quantitative, because a coach is an expert.
items.random_wrong: It is primary data, so it must be numerical.
items.why: Quality of data is set by its form (numbers vs description), not by who collected it.

items.q: What is the difference between primary and secondary data?
items.correct: Primary is collected first-hand by you; secondary is collected by someone else.
items.misconception: Primary data is always more important than secondary.
items.random_wrong: They are the same if both are numbers.
items.why: The split is about who gathered the data, not its value or whether it is numerical.

items.q: How do you calculate the mean of a data set?
items.correct: Add all the values together and divide by the number of values.
items.misconception: Subtract the smallest value from the largest.
items.random_wrong: Pick the value that appears most often.
items.why: Mean is the average; range is largest minus smallest and mode is the most common value.

items.q: Heart rate measured in beats per minute is an example of:
items.why: It's Quantitative data. “Qualitative data” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A coach's written description of a player's attitude is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Qualitative data. “Quantitative data” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Data you collect yourself during a fitness test is:
items.why: It's Primary data. “Secondary data” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The number of goals scored in a match is an example of:
items.why: It's Discrete data. “Continuous data” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The resting heart rates of five athletes are 60, 64, 62, 66 and 68 bpm. What is the mean?
items.why: Exact answer: 64 bpm. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
